expanded_description: Understanding solubility curves—graphs that show how the solubility of a substance changes with temperature—and learning to interpret these graphs to predict saturation states, crystallization, and solution behavior.

core_concept.principle: Solubility curves graphically represent the maximum amount of solute that can dissolve in a solvent at different temperatures, allowing prediction of saturation states and crystallization behavior.
core_concept.explanation: A solubility curve plots solubility (typically in g/100 g water) on the y-axis versus temperature on the x-axis. Points on the curve represent saturated solutions; points below represent unsaturated solutions; points above represent supersaturated (unstable) solutions. These curves enable quantitative predictions about how much solute will dissolve or crystallize when temperature changes.

image.alt: Graph showing solubility curves for KNO3, NaCl, and Ca(OH)2 versus temperature
image.caption: Solubility curves show how different substances respond differently to temperature changes

sections.introduction: Solubility curves are essential tools in chemistry that visualize the relationship between temperature and solubility. By interpreting these graphs, you can predict whether a solution is saturated, determine how much solute will crystallize upon cooling, and compare the solubility behavior of different substances.

key_concept.definition: A solubility curve is a graph that plots the maximum mass of solute that can dissolve in 100 g of solvent (usually water) at each temperature, forming a line that represents the saturation boundary.
key_concept.context: Each substance has its own unique solubility curve. The shape of the curve reveals whether solubility increases, decreases, or remains relatively constant with temperature.

principles.statement: Points on the solubility curve represent saturated solutions; points below represent unsaturated solutions; points above represent supersaturated solutions.
principles.application: This principle allows you to determine the state of any solution by plotting its concentration and temperature on the graph.
principles.example: At $40^\circ\text{C}$, if the curve shows $60$ g/100 g water for $\text{KNO}_3$, a solution with $40$ g/100 g is unsaturated, $60$ g/100 g is saturated, and $80$ g/100 g is supersaturated.
principles.analogy: Like a capacity line on a graph—below the line there's room for more; on the line it's full; above the line it's overflowing.

principles.statement: The slope of a solubility curve indicates how strongly solubility depends on temperature.
principles.application: Steep positive slope: solubility increases rapidly with temperature (good for recrystallization); shallow slope: weak temperature dependence; negative slope: solubility decreases with temperature (exception).
principles.example: $\text{KNO}_3$ has a steep positive curve (solubility increases dramatically with $T$); $\text{NaCl}$ has a shallow curve (weak dependence); $\text{Ca(OH)}_2$ has a negative slope (solubility decreases with $T$).
principles.analogy: Like different hills—some are steep (big change with small temperature change), some are flat (little change).

components.definition: Solubility curves have specific axes, units, and features that must be understood for correct interpretation.
components.equation: $\text{Solubility} = f(T)$ where solubility is typically in g solute/100 g $\text{H}_2\text{O}$ and $T$ is in $^\circ\text{C}$
components.interpretation: Read the graph by finding the temperature on the x-axis, moving vertically to the curve, then horizontally to read solubility on the y-axis.

image.alt: Diagram showing how to read a solubility curve with arrows
image.caption: To find solubility at a temperature: go up from x-axis to curve, then left to y-axis

worked_examples.title: Reading a Solubility Curve
worked_examples.problem: Using a solubility curve for $\text{KNO}_3$, find the solubility at $30^\circ\text{C}$ and at $70^\circ\text{C}$. If the curve shows $45$ g/100 g water at $30^\circ\text{C}$ and $135$ g/100 g water at $70^\circ\text{C}$, how much more $\text{KNO}_3$ can dissolve at the higher temperature?

step_by_step.explanation: Find $30^\circ\text{C}$ on x-axis, go up to $\text{KNO}_3$ curve, read y-value
step_by_step.calculation: Solubility at $30^\circ\text{C}$: $45$ g/100 g water

step_by_step.explanation: Find $70^\circ\text{C}$ on x-axis, go up to curve, read y-value
step_by_step.calculation: Solubility at $70^\circ\text{C}$: $135$ g/100 g water

step_by_step.explanation: Subtract lower temperature solubility from higher temperature solubility
step_by_step.calculation: Difference = $135 \text{ g} - 45 \text{ g} = 90 \text{ g}$ more $\text{KNO}_3$ can dissolve per 100 g water

worked_examples.final_answer: At $70^\circ\text{C}$, $90$ g more $\text{KNO}_3$ can dissolve per 100 g water than at $30^\circ\text{C}$
worked_examples.concept_applied: Solubility curves allow quantitative comparison of solubility at different temperatures.

worked_examples.title: Identifying Solution State from Curve
worked_examples.problem: A solution contains $80$ g $\text{KNO}_3$ in $100$ g water at $50^\circ\text{C}$. If the solubility curve shows $85$ g/100 g water at $50^\circ\text{C}$, is the solution unsaturated, saturated, or supersaturated?

step_by_step.step: Find the saturation point on the curve
step_by_step.explanation: At $50^\circ\text{C}$, the curve shows maximum solubility = $85$ g/100 g water
step_by_step.calculation: Saturation limit: $85$ g/100 g water

step_by_step.step: Compare actual concentration to saturation limit
step_by_step.explanation: Actual = $80$ g/100 g water; Saturation = $85$ g/100 g water
step_by_step.calculation: $80 \text{ g} < 85 \text{ g}$

step_by_step.explanation: Since actual < saturation limit, solution can hold more solute
step_by_step.calculation: State: Unsaturated

worked_examples.final_answer: The solution is unsaturated (can dissolve $5$ g more $\text{KNO}_3$ per 100 g water)
worked_examples.concept_applied: Compare actual concentration to curve value to determine saturation state.

sections.key_concept: Solubility curves provide a visual method to determine whether a solution is unsaturated, saturated, or supersaturated by comparing the actual concentration to the curve value at a given temperature.

classifications.value: Points below the solubility curve represent unsaturated solutions
characteristics: Concentration < curve value at that temperature
classifications.behavior: Adding more solute will result in further dissolution until the curve is reached

examples.explanation: If curve shows $60$ g at $40^\circ\text{C}$, then $30$ g is below curve → unsaturated

classifications.value: Points on the solubility curve represent saturated solutions at equilibrium
classifications.behavior: Adding more solute results in no further dissolution; excess remains as solid

examples.explanation: Matches curve value exactly → saturated; undissolved solid may be present

classifications.value: Points above the solubility curve represent supersaturated (metastable) solutions
classifications.behavior: Adding a seed crystal or disturbing the solution causes rapid crystallization of excess solute

examples.explanation: Above curve ($60$ g at $40^\circ\text{C}$) → supersaturated; will crystallize if seeded

worked_examples.title: Determining State from Graph Coordinates
worked_examples.problem: A $\text{NaCl}$ solution has concentration $37$ g/100 g water at $60^\circ\text{C}$. If the solubility curve shows $37.5$ g/100 g water at $60^\circ\text{C}$, what is the solution state? What happens if cooled to $20^\circ\text{C}$ where solubility is $36.0$ g/100 g water?

step_by_step.explanation: Actual ($37$ g) < curve ($37.5$ g) at $60^\circ\text{C}$
step_by_step.calculation: State at $60^\circ\text{C}$: Unsaturated

step_by_step.explanation: Actual ($37$ g) > curve ($36.0$ g) at $20^\circ\text{C}$
step_by_step.calculation: State at $20^\circ\text{C}$: Supersaturated (if no crystallization occurs)

step_by_step.explanation: Excess beyond saturation limit will crystallize
step_by_step.calculation: Crystallized = $37.0 \text{ g} - 36.0 \text{ g} = 1.0 \text{ g NaCl per 100 g water}$

worked_examples.final_answer: At $60^\circ\text{C}$: unsaturated; at $20^\circ\text{C}$: supersaturated with $1.0$ g crystallizing per 100 g water
worked_examples.concept_applied: Cooling can change solution state from unsaturated to supersaturated, causing crystallization.

worked_examples.title: Comparing Multiple Substances on One Graph
worked_examples.problem: A solubility graph shows curves for $\text{KNO}_3$, $\text{NaCl}$, and $\text{Ca(OH)}_2$. At $50^\circ\text{C}$, which substance has the highest solubility? Which has solubility that decreases with temperature?

step_by_step.explanation: Find $50^\circ\text{C}$ on x-axis; read y-values where each curve intersects
step_by_step.calculation: Typical values: $\text{KNO}_3$ ≈ $85$ g; $\text{NaCl}$ ≈ $37$ g; $\text{Ca(OH)}_2$ ≈ $0.13$ g

step_by_step.explanation: Higher y-value = higher solubility
step_by_step.calculation: Highest solubility at $50^\circ\text{C}$: $\text{KNO}_3$

step_by_step.explanation: Curve slope indicates temperature dependence
step_by_step.calculation: $\text{KNO}_3$: steep positive slope (↑ solubility with ↑ $T$); $\text{NaCl}$: shallow slope (weak dependence); $\text{Ca(OH)}_2$: negative slope (↓ solubility with ↑ $T$)

worked_examples.final_answer: Highest solubility at $50^\circ\text{C}$: $\text{KNO}_3$; Solubility decreases with temperature: $\text{Ca(OH)}_2$
worked_examples.concept_applied: Solubility curves enable direct comparison of different substances' temperature dependence.

sections.introduction: One of the most practical applications of solubility curves is predicting how much solute will crystallize when a saturated solution is cooled. This principle underlies recrystallization purification techniques used in laboratories and industry.

key_concept.definition: When a saturated solution is cooled, the amount of solute that crystallizes equals the difference between the initial solubility (at high temperature) and the final solubility (at low temperature).
key_concept.context: This calculation assumes no solvent evaporation and that crystallization proceeds to equilibrium. The mass of solvent remains constant during cooling.

principles.statement: Mass crystallized = (Initial solubility - Final solubility) × (Mass of solvent / 100 g)
principles.application: This formula allows calculation of expected crystal yield from temperature change and solvent mass.
principles.example: Cooling $200$ g water saturated with $\text{KNO}_3$ from $70^\circ\text{C}$ ($135$ g/100 g) to $20^\circ\text{C}$ ($32$ g/100 g): Crystallized = $(135 - 32) \times (200/100) = 206$ g $\text{KNO}_3$.
principles.analogy: Like a sponge holding water—when you squeeze (cool), the excess water (solute) comes out.

worked_examples.title: Calculating Crystallization from Curve Data
worked_examples.problem: A saturated solution of $\text{KNO}_3$ at $60^\circ\text{C}$ contains $110$ g $\text{KNO}_3$ in $100$ g water. If cooled to $20^\circ\text{C}$ where solubility is $32$ g/100 g water, how much $\text{KNO}_3$ will crystallize?

step_by_step.explanation: From curve: at $60^\circ\text{C}$: $110$ g/100 g water; at $20^\circ\text{C}$: $32$ g/100 g water
step_by_step.calculation: Initial: $110$ g; Final: $32$ g (per 100 g water)

step_by_step.explanation: Excess beyond final solubility crystallizes
step_by_step.calculation: Crystallized = $110 \text{ g} - 32 \text{ g} = 78 \text{ g KNO}_3$ per 100 g water

step_by_step.explanation: Cooling causes $78$ g of $\text{KNO}_3$ to crystallize from each 100 g of water
step_by_step.calculation: Remaining dissolved: $32$ g; Crystallized: $78$ g

worked_examples.final_answer: $78$ g of $\text{KNO}_3$ will crystallize per 100 g water when cooled from $60^\circ\text{C}$ to $20^\circ\text{C}$
worked_examples.concept_applied: Crystallization mass = difference in solubility values at initial and final temperatures.

worked_examples.title: Scaling Crystallization to Different Solvent Mass
worked_examples.problem: How much $\text{KNO}_3$ crystallizes when $250$ g of water saturated at $60^\circ\text{C}$ is cooled to $20^\circ\text{C}$? (Solubilities: $110$ g/100 g at $60^\circ\text{C}$; $32$ g/100 g at $20^\circ\text{C}$)

step_by_step.explanation: Difference in solubilities
step_by_step.calculation: Per 100 g: $110 \text{ g} - 32 \text{ g} = 78 \text{ g crystallized}$

step_by_step.explanation: Multiply by (actual solvent mass / 100 g)
step_by_step.calculation: Crystallized = $78 \text{ g} \times \dfrac{250 \text{ g water}}{100 \text{ g water}} = 195 \text{ g KNO}_3$

step_by_step.explanation: Initial dissolved: $110 \times 2.5 = 275$ g; Final dissolved: $32 \times 2.5 = 80$ g
step_by_step.calculation: Crystallized = $275 \text{ g} - 80 \text{ g} = 195 \text{ g}$ ✓

worked_examples.final_answer: $195$ g of $\text{KNO}_3$ will crystallize from $250$ g water
worked_examples.concept_applied: Crystallization scales linearly with solvent mass.

sections.key_concept: Different substances exhibit characteristic solubility curve shapes that reflect their dissolution thermodynamics. Recognizing these patterns helps predict behavior without memorizing specific values.

categories.detailed_mechanism: Dissolution is strongly endothermic ($\Delta H_{soln} \gg 0$); heat acts as reactant; Le Chatelier's principle favors dissolution at higher $T$

examples.explanation: Solubility may increase 3-5× over $0-100^\circ\text{C}$ range; excellent for recrystallization purification
Large solubility change enables efficient purification: dissolve at high $T$, crystallize at low $T$

categories.detailed_mechanism: Dissolution is weakly endothermic ($\Delta H_{soln} \approx 0$); temperature has minimal effect on equilibrium

examples.explanation: Solubility changes <15% over $0-100^\circ\text{C}$; recrystallization is inefficient
Evaporation, not cooling, is preferred for recovering these salts from solution

categories.detailed_mechanism: Dissolution is exothermic ($\Delta H_{soln} < 0$); heat is product; increasing $T$ shifts equilibrium toward solid

examples.explanation: $\text{Ca(OH)}_2$ solubility: $0.185$ g/100 g at $0^\circ\text{C}$ vs. $0.077$ g/100 g at $100^\circ\text{C}$
Heating a saturated solution causes precipitation; cooling increases solubility

categories.description: Solubility shows a maximum or minimum due to phase transitions
categories.detailed_mechanism: Change in hydrate form or crystal structure alters dissolution thermodynamics at transition temperature

examples.explanation: Solubility increases to $32.4^\circ\text{C}$ (decahydrate stable), then decreases (anhydrous form stable)
Recrystallization requires careful temperature control around transition point

worked_examples.title: Identifying Curve Type from Shape
worked_examples.problem: A solubility curve for substance X shows solubility increasing from $20$ g/100 g at $20^\circ\text{C}$ to $180$ g/100 g at $80^\circ\text{C}$. What type of curve is this, and what does it imply about purification?

step_by_step.explanation: Solubility increases 9× over $60^\circ\text{C}$ range
step_by_step.calculation: Slope: steep positive

step_by_step.explanation: Steep positive slope indicates strongly endothermic dissolution
step_by_step.calculation: Type: Steep positive slope curve

step_by_step.explanation: Large solubility change enables efficient recrystallization
step_by_step.calculation: Purification: Highly efficient; dissolve at $80^\circ\text{C}$, cool to $20^\circ\text{C}$ to crystallize

worked_examples.final_answer: Steep positive slope curve; excellent for recrystallization purification
worked_examples.concept_applied: Curve shape predicts both thermodynamic behavior and practical purification efficiency.

worked_examples.title: Interpreting a Negative Slope Curve
worked_examples.problem: The solubility curve for $\text{Ca(OH)}_2$ slopes downward. If you have a saturated solution at $20^\circ\text{C}$ and heat it to $80^\circ\text{C}$, what happens?

step_by_step.step: Recall the meaning of negative slope
step_by_step.explanation: Solubility decreases as temperature increases
step_by_step.calculation: Higher $T$ → lower solubility

step_by_step.explanation: Heating reduces the amount of $\text{Ca(OH)}_2$ that can remain dissolved
step_by_step.calculation: Excess $\text{Ca(OH)}_2$ will precipitate as temperature rises

step_by_step.explanation: Dissolution is exothermic; adding heat shifts equilibrium toward solid
step_by_step.calculation: Le Chatelier's principle: heat is product; adding heat favors reactants (solid)

worked_examples.final_answer: Heating causes $\text{Ca(OH)}_2$ to precipitate because its solubility decreases with temperature
worked_examples.concept_applied: Negative slope curves represent exothermic dissolution; heating causes precipitation.

sections.key_concept: Solubility curves are not just academic tools—they guide real-world processes from laboratory purification to industrial crystallization and environmental management.

classifications.value: Using temperature-dependent solubility to purify solids
characteristics: Dissolve impure solid at high $T$ where solubility is high
Filter hot to remove insoluble impurities
classifications.behavior: Effective for substances with steep positive solubility curves; inefficient for shallow or negative slope curves

examples.explanation: Dissolve in minimum hot water; cool to crystallize pure $\text{KNO}_3$; impurities remain in solution or are filtered hot

classifications.value: Creating solutions with concentration above equilibrium solubility
characteristics: Dissolve at high $T$, cool carefully without disturbance
classifications.behavior: Used in hand warmers, rock candy production, and nucleation demonstrations

examples.explanation: Supersaturated solution crystallizes upon clicking metal disk, releasing heat of crystallization

classifications.value: Scaling laboratory recrystallization to industrial production
characteristics: Use solubility curves to design cooling profiles
classifications.behavior: Curve data informs reactor design, cooling rates, and yield predictions

examples.explanation: Solubility curves guide crystallization conditions to obtain desired crystal form and purity

classifications.value: Predicting mineral precipitation in natural waters
characteristics: Temperature changes in lakes/rivers affect mineral solubility
Seasonal variations influence scaling and sedimentation
classifications.behavior: Solubility curves help predict when and where minerals will precipitate

examples.explanation: $\text{CaCO}_3$ solubility decreases with warming; precipitation forms stalactites/stalagmites

worked_examples.title: Designing a Recrystallization Procedure Using Curves
worked_examples.problem: You have $15.0$ g of impure benzoic acid. Solubility curve shows: $0.34$ g/100 g water at $25^\circ\text{C}$; $6.80$ g/100 g water at $95^\circ\text{C}$. Design a recrystallization procedure and calculate expected recovery.

step_by_step.step: Calculate minimum solvent at high temperature
step_by_step.explanation: At $95^\circ\text{C}$, solubility is $6.80$ g/100 g water
step_by_step.calculation: Water needed = $15.0 \text{ g} \times \dfrac{100 \text{ g water}}{6.80 \text{ g}} = 221 \text{ g water} \approx 221 \text{ mL}$

step_by_step.explanation: Heat $221$ mL water to $95^\circ\text{C}$; add benzoic acid until dissolved; filter hot to remove insoluble impurities
step_by_step.calculation: Use slight excess solvent to ensure complete dissolution

step_by_step.explanation: At $25^\circ\text{C}$, solubility is $0.34$ g/100 g water
step_by_step.calculation: Dissolved at $25^\circ\text{C}$: $0.34 \times 2.21 = 0.75$ g; Crystallized: $15.0 - 0.75 = 14.25$ g

step_by_step.explanation: Recovery = (crystallized / initial) × 100%
step_by_step.calculation: Recovery = $(14.25 / 15.0) \times 100\% = 95\%$ (theoretical; actual lower due to losses)

worked_examples.final_answer: Use ~$225$ mL hot water; expect ~$14.3$ g pure benzoic acid ($95\%$ theoretical recovery)
worked_examples.concept_applied: Solubility curves enable quantitative design of recrystallization procedures.

worked_examples.title: Predicting Mineral Precipitation in a Lake
worked_examples.problem: A lake contains $0.020$ g $\text{Ca(OH)}_2$/100 g water at $10^\circ\text{C}$. If summer warming raises water temperature to $30^\circ\text{C}$, will $\text{Ca(OH)}_2$ precipitate? (Solubility: $0.185$ g/100 g at $0^\circ\text{C}$; $0.145$ g/100 g at $20^\circ\text{C}$; $0.077$ g/100 g at $100^\circ\text{C}$)

step_by_step.explanation: Interpolate between given points; curve has negative slope
step_by_step.calculation: At $10^\circ\text{C}$: ~$0.165$ g/100 g; At $30^\circ\text{C}$: ~$0.130$ g/100 g (estimated)

step_by_step.explanation: Actual = $0.020$ g/100 g at $10^\circ\text{C}$
step_by_step.calculation: $0.020 \text{ g} < 0.165 \text{ g}$ → unsaturated at $10^\circ\text{C}$

step_by_step.explanation: At $30^\circ\text{C}$, solubility (~$0.130$ g) still > actual ($0.020$ g)
step_by_step.calculation: Solution remains unsaturated; no precipitation expected

step_by_step.explanation: Even with warming, concentration is far below saturation; $\text{Ca(OH)}_2$ precipitation not a concern
step_by_step.calculation: Only if concentration approached $0.130$ g/100 g would warming cause precipitation

worked_examples.final_answer: No precipitation expected; actual concentration ($0.020$ g/100 g) remains below solubility even at $30^\circ\text{C}$
worked_examples.concept_applied: Solubility curves enable prediction of mineral behavior in natural systems under temperature change.

sections.key_concept: Misreading solubility curves is a common source of error. Understanding typical mistakes helps ensure accurate interpretation and predictions.

classifications.value: Confusing x-axis (temperature) with y-axis (solubility) or misinterpreting units
characteristics: Reading temperature as solubility or vice versa
classifications.behavior: Always verify axis labels and units before reading values; use a ruler for accurate interpolation

examples.process: Reading $40$ as solubility when it's temperature
examples.explanation: Always trace from x-axis up to curve, then left to y-axis

classifications.type: Error: Assuming All Curves Have Positive Slope
classifications.value: Applying 'solubility increases with temperature' to all substances
classifications.behavior: Check the curve's slope before predicting temperature effects; negative slope means opposite behavior

examples.explanation: Wrong: 'Heating dissolves more $\text{Ca(OH)}_2$'; Correct: heating causes precipitation

classifications.type: Error: Ignoring Solvent Mass in Crystallization
classifications.value: Calculating crystallized mass without scaling to actual solvent amount
characteristics: Using per-100-g values directly for different solvent masses
Forgetting to multiply by (actual mass/100 g)
classifications.behavior: Always scale crystallization calculation: Mass crystallized = (Δsolubility) × (actual solvent mass/100 g)

examples.explanation: If curve difference is $78$ g/100 g, actual crystallized = $78 \times 2.5 = 195$ g, not $78$ g

classifications.value: Treating points above the curve as stable saturated solutions
classifications.behavior: Points above curve represent unstable supersaturated solutions; they crystallize upon disturbance

examples.explanation: Solution appears clear but crystallizes instantly when seeded or shaken

worked_examples.title: Error Analysis: Unit Misinterpretation
worked_examples.problem: A student reads a solubility curve and reports that $\text{KNO}_3$ solubility at $50^\circ\text{C}$ is $50$ g/L. The curve actually shows $85$ g/100 g water. Identify and correct the error.

step_by_step.explanation: Student misread units or confused g/100 g with g/L
step_by_step.calculation: Curve units: g solute/100 g $\text{H}_2\text{O}$, not g/L

step_by_step.explanation: At $50^\circ\text{C}$, curve shows $85$ g $\text{KNO}_3$ per 100 g water
step_by_step.calculation: Correct value: $85$ g/100 g water

step_by_step.explanation: If g/L is required, use water density (~$1$ g/mL)
step_by_step.calculation: $85$ g/100 g water ≈ $85$ g/100 mL = $850$ g/L (approximate)

step_by_step.explanation: Always verify units on graph axes before reporting values
step_by_step.calculation: Units matter: g/100 g water ≠ g/L

worked_examples.final_answer: Error: misread units; correct solubility = $85$ g/100 g water at $50^\circ\text{C}$
worked_examples.concept_applied: Always verify axis labels and units when reading solubility curves.

worked_examples.title: Solubility Curve Interpretation Checklist
worked_examples.problem: Outline a systematic approach for interpreting solubility curves.

step_by_step.explanation: Confirm x-axis = temperature ($^\circ\text{C}$), y-axis = solubility (g/100 g water or other)
Use legend or labels to select correct curve if multiple substances are plotted
Use ruler or straight edge to trace from x-axis to curve to y-axis
step_by_step.calculation: Interpolate between grid lines if needed

step_by_step.explanation: Compare actual concentration to curve value: below = unsaturated; on = saturated; above = supersaturated
Crystallized = (Initial solubility - Final solubility) × (solvent mass/100 g)
step_by_step.calculation: Scale to actual solvent mass

step_by_step.step: Step 6: Check curve slope for temperature dependence
step_by_step.explanation: Positive slope: solubility ↑ with ↑ $T$; Negative slope: solubility ↓ with ↑ $T$
step_by_step.calculation: Use slope to predict behavior at unlisted temperatures

worked_examples.final_answer: Follow 6-step checklist: verify axes, identify curve, read accurately, determine state, calculate crystallization, check slope
worked_examples.concept_applied: Systematic approach prevents common solubility curve interpretation errors.

sections.introduction: Test your understanding with these practice problems. Try solving before looking at solutions.

key_concept.definition: Practice problems reinforce solubility curve interpretation skills across different scenarios.
key_concept.context: Cover solutions and attempt each problem independently, then check your work.

worked_examples.title: Practice Problem 1
worked_examples.problem: A solubility curve shows $\text{NH}_4\text{Cl}$ solubility of $40$ g/100 g water at $30^\circ\text{C}$ and $75$ g/100 g water at $70^\circ\text{C}$. Is a solution with $50$ g $\text{NH}_4\text{Cl}$ in $100$ g water at $50^\circ\text{C}$ unsaturated, saturated, or supersaturated? (Assume linear change between given points)

step_by_step.explanation: Linear interpolation: midpoint between $30^\circ\text{C}$ and $70^\circ\text{C}$
step_by_step.calculation: Solubility at $50^\circ\text{C}$ ≈ $(40 + 75)/2 = 57.5$ g/100 g water

step_by_step.explanation: Actual = $50$ g/100 g; Estimated saturation = $57.5$ g/100 g
step_by_step.calculation: $50 \text{ g} < 57.5 \text{ g}$

step_by_step.explanation: Actual < saturation limit
step_by_step.calculation: State: Unsaturated

worked_examples.final_answer: Unsaturated (can dissolve $7.5$ g more $\text{NH}_4\text{Cl}$ per 100 g water at $50^\circ\text{C}$)
worked_examples.concept_applied: Interpolate curve values to determine solution state at intermediate temperatures.

worked_examples.title: Practice Problem 2
worked_examples.problem: Using the same $\text{NH}_4\text{Cl}$ curve, how much $\text{NH}_4\text{Cl}$ crystallizes when $150$ g of water saturated at $70^\circ\text{C}$ is cooled to $30^\circ\text{C}$?

step_by_step.explanation: At $70^\circ\text{C}$: $75$ g/100 g; At $30^\circ\text{C}$: $40$ g/100 g
step_by_step.calculation: ΔSolubility = $75 - 40 = 35$ g/100 g water

step_by_step.explanation: Multiply by (actual water mass / 100 g)
step_by_step.calculation: Crystallized = $35 \text{ g} \times \dfrac{150 \text{ g water}}{100 \text{ g water}} = 52.5 \text{ g NH}_4\text{Cl}$

worked_examples.concept_applied: Crystallization scales with solvent mass; use curve values to calculate yield.

worked_examples.title: Practice Problem 3
worked_examples.problem: A curve for substance Y shows solubility decreasing from $10$ g/100 g at $10^\circ\text{C}$ to $2$ g/100 g at $90^\circ\text{C}$. What happens when a saturated solution at $10^\circ\text{C}$ is heated to $90^\circ\text{C}$?

step_by_step.explanation: Solubility decreases with increasing temperature
step_by_step.calculation: Negative slope curve

step_by_step.explanation: Heating reduces solubility; excess solute crystallizes
step_by_step.calculation: Crystallized = $10 \text{ g} - 2 \text{ g} = 8$ g per 100 g water

worked_examples.final_answer: Heating causes $8$ g of substance Y to crystallize per 100 g water
worked_examples.concept_applied: Negative slope curves: heating causes precipitation, not dissolution.

worked_examples.title: Practice Problem 4
worked_examples.problem: A solution contains $65$ g $\text{KNO}_3$ in $100$ g water at $40^\circ\text{C}$. The curve shows solubility of $60$ g/100 g at $40^\circ\text{C}$. What is the solution state, and what happens if a seed crystal is added?

step_by_step.explanation: Actual ($65$ g) > curve ($60$ g) at $40^\circ\text{C}$
step_by_step.calculation: State: Supersaturated

step_by_step.explanation: Supersaturated solutions crystallize upon disturbance or seeding
step_by_step.calculation: Adding seed crystal triggers rapid crystallization of excess $5$ g $\text{KNO}_3$

worked_examples.final_answer: Supersaturated; adding seed crystal causes $5$ g $\text{KNO}_3$ to crystallize
worked_examples.concept_applied: Points above curve represent supersaturated solutions that crystallize upon seeding.

worked_examples.title: Practice Problem 5
worked_examples.problem: A solubility curve has three substances: A (steep positive slope), B (shallow positive slope), C (negative slope). Which is best purified by recrystallization? Which precipitates when heated?

step_by_step.explanation: A: steep positive; B: shallow positive; C: negative
Steep positive slope = large solubility change = efficient recrystallization
step_by_step.calculation: Best for recrystallization: Substance A

step_by_step.explanation: Negative slope = solubility decreases with heating = precipitation upon heating
step_by_step.calculation: Precipitates when heated: Substance C

worked_examples.final_answer: Best for recrystallization: A; Precipitates when heated: C
worked_examples.concept_applied: Curve slope predicts both purification efficiency and temperature response.

summary: Solubility curves graphically represent the maximum solute concentration (typically g/100 g water) that can dissolve at each temperature. Key principles: (1) Points on the curve = saturated solutions; below = unsaturated; above = supersaturated (unstable). (2) Curve slope indicates temperature dependence: steep positive = solubility increases rapidly with $T$ (good for recrystallization); shallow positive = weak dependence; negative = solubility decreases with $T$ (exceptions like $\text{Ca(OH)}_2$). (3) Crystallization upon cooling = (Initial solubility - Final solubility) × (solvent mass/100 g). Reading curves requires: verifying axes/units, identifying the correct substance curve, accurate interpolation, comparing actual concentration to curve value, and scaling calculations to actual solvent mass. Common errors include: misreading axes or units, assuming all curves have positive slope, ignoring solvent mass in crystallization calculations, and confusing supersaturated with saturated states. A systematic 6-step interpretation checklist ensures accuracy: verify axes, identify curve, read accurately, determine state, calculate crystallization, check slope. Practical applications include recrystallization purification, supersaturated solution preparation, industrial crystallization design, and environmental mineral precipitation predictions. Mastery of solubility curve interpretation enables quantitative prediction of solution behavior under temperature changes, essential for laboratory work, industrial processes, and understanding natural systems.
